coredata.dc:title: Sliver nanoparticles@carbon dots for synergistic antibacterial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Panyong
Song, Yizhi
Mei, Qian
Dong, Wen-Fei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: A novel composite material named sliver nanoparticles@carbon dots (AgNPs/CDs) was fabricated with CDs as the capping agents through one simple method. The images of TEM showed that CDs were embedded in the gaps of AgNPs and the as-prepared AgNPs/CDs can be self assembled into nanochains through electrosatic interaction between silver nanoparticles and carbon dots. With higher redox properties than AgNPs, the as-prepared composites exhibited excellent synergistic antibacterial performance against Gram-positive bacterium and Gram-negative bacterium in the antibacterial assays, which indicates potential application as an effective antibacterial material. And the results of antibacterial assy showed that the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of AgNPs/CDs were 40 μg/mL for E. coli and 20 μg/mL for S. aureus in LB medium. In addition, the oxidative stress induced by AgNPs/CDs-mediated reactive oxygen species further confirmed good application prospects in antibacterial.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgNPs
Carbon dots
ROS
Composites
Antibacterial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ag NPs and AgNPs/CDs
Antibacterial effect of the Ag NPs/CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of CDs (a), AgNPs (b) and AgNPs/CDs (c). The correspongding diamter sizes of CDs (d), AgNPs (e) and AgNPs/CDs (f).
UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs (a), AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs (b).
XPS analysis of the AgNPs/CDs (a), peak positions of C 1s (b), O 1s (c) and Ag 3d (d).
FT-IR spectra of CDs, AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs.
Growth curves of E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b) treated with AgNPs/CDs at different concentrations.
Images of bacterial colonies against of E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b) after being treated with AgNPs/CDs at different concentrations.
Scanning electron microscopy images of E. coli (a), E. coli incubated with AgNPs/CDs (b), S. aureus (c) and S. aureus incubated with AgNPs/CDs (d).
Images of antibacterial effect of materials toward E. coli (a) and S. aureus (b).
Schematic of the antibacterial mechanism of AgNPs/CDs.
Schematic illustration of preparing AgNPs/CDs and their synergistic antibacterial mechanism.

head.ce:title: Sliver nanoparticles@carbon dots for synergistic antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel composite material named sliver nanoparticles@carbon dots (AgNPs/CDs) was fabricated with CDs as the capping agents through one simple method. The images of TEM showed that CDs were embedded in the gaps of AgNPs and the as-prepared AgNPs/CDs can be self assembled into nanochains through electrosatic interaction between silver nanoparticles and carbon dots. With higher redox properties than AgNPs, the as-prepared composites exhibited excellent synergistic antibacterial performance against Gram-positive bacterium and Gram-negative bacterium in the antibacterial assays, which indicates potential application as an effective antibacterial material. And the results of antibacterial assy showed that the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of AgNPs/CDs were 40 μg/mL for E. coli and 20 μg/mL for S. aureus in LB medium. In addition, the oxidative stress induced by AgNPs/CDs-mediated reactive oxygen species further confirmed good application prospects in antibacterial.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection has become increasingly common and a major global health concern with the growing antibiotic resistance of bacteria[1,2]. In the world, it’s supposed that 700,000 people are killed by drug-resistant bacteria annually[3]. The estimated societal cost of treating drug-resistant infections is approximately $20 billion annually[4]. As a result of genetic changes, bacteria have developed resistance to antibiotics, and the crisis has been exacerbated by the unjustified use of antibacterial agents[5]. Therefore, it’s of great importance to design effective, biocompatible and long-term antibacterial materials to reduce the spread of drug-resistant bacteria.

Nanomaterials have become an innovative alternative therapy against drug-resistant bacteria when coventional anbitiotics fail[6–8]. Compared with the traditional materials in bulk form, as the size decreases to the nanoscale (10-9 m), the antibacterial effect of nanomaterials increases with the larger total surface area unit volume[9]. This new antibacterial field includes many nanomaterials, such as metal ions[10], graphene oxide[11,12] and dendrimers[13]. Silver-based agents have gained much interest due to their tremendous potential and significant antibacterial applications in lots of pathogenic and drug-resistant infections[14–17]. They possess a very high antimicrobial effect against broad-spectrum microbes around 650 organisms (bacteria, yeasts and viruses)[18]. Because of its excellent antibacterial properities, AgNPs have been applied into many fields of healthcare including delivery of threapeutic agents[19], skin wound healing[20], photocatalyst and bone grafts[21]. However, the traditional silver nanoparticles are easily agglomerated with poor dispersion and rapidly oxidized to silver ions, which limits its applications. Carbon dots (CDs), as the zero-dimensional carbon materials, have been paid much attention for easy surface functionalization, facile synthesis and photochemical properties[22,23]. With the good oxidation–reduction performance and unique photoelectric properties, CDs can serve as an agent to inhibit the growth of bacteria and are considered promising nanomaterials for application in biosafety[24–26]. Bing and co-workers demonstrated the mechanism of E.coli death after incubation with charged CDs and the generated ROS became the major factor for antibacterial[27]. Li and co-workers synthesized degradable CDs with the efficient activity of inhibiting growth of microorganisms[28]. In addition, carbon-based nanomaterials have been used as subatrate nanomaterials for SERS because of the small size and unique optical properities [29]. Compare with the most common Au/Ag NPs used as SERS substrate, the stronger localized surface plasmon was created by AgNPs/CDs resonance due to the effects of aggregation dimension[30]. A great deal of work has been reported on AgNPs/CDs for the surface-enhanced Raman scattering application, such as measurement of chemical molecules in the catalytic process, the identification of bacterial species in medical diagnostics and detection of pesticides in environmental samples[31–34]. To our knowledge, only a few studies have focused on the application of AgNPs/CDs in antibacterial.

In this work, nanochain AgNPs/CDs with high-performance antibacterial was prepared by using CDs through one simple method (Scheme 1 ). Then, the morphology and chemical structure of composites AgNPs/CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR and XPS. Furthermore, the antibacterial activities of the synthesized AgNPs/CDs were determined against Gram-positive bacterium and Gram-negative bacterium. And the generation of ROS stimulated by AgNPs/CDs promoted cell death. Lastly, compared with commercial metal nanoparticles, AgNPs/CDs showed stronger antibacterial effect against S. aureus and E. coli, which indicates it had great potential as an effective antibacterial agent to inhibit the growth of bacteria.

Sliver nitrate, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and artemisinin were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (China). All regents were not processed or purified prior to use. Glucose, acetic acid and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Beijing China). Luria-Bertani broth and Luria-Bertani agar were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Eacherichia coil was kindly provided by Prof. Yizhi Song group. Staphylococcus was purchased from Shanghai Luwei Technology Co. Ltd.

The synthesis of CDs was similar to our reported method[35]. In brief, 0.03 g artemisinin was dissolved in 5 mL acetic acid and 25 mL ultrapure water. Clear solution was obtained by ultrasonic oscillation for 10 min. The solution was subject to hydrothermal heating at 200 ℃ for 6 h in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. The obtained product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 8 min 2 times to separate the large particles and the residue was separated by filtration with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to further detach the aggregates (Mw = 1000 Da) for 8 h. The product was lyophilized to obtain solid of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ag NPs and AgNPs/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-coated sliver nanoparticles were synthesized accoding to previous work[36]. The AgNPs/CDs was fabricated through a modified one-pot reaction. First, 1 g glucose and 0.5 g PVP were dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water and heated to 100 ℃ with a heating jacket and hold for 5 min. The 0.3 g silver nitrate dissolved in 1 mL of water and 1 mL CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was added to the sliver nitrate solution, and incubated for 10 min. Then the mixture was quickly added into the necked flask with mechanical stirring for an additional 3 h. The as-prepared silver nanoparticles modified by CDs (AgNPs/CDs) were washed in ultrapure water three times via ultracentrifugation (30000 rpm, 30 min) to remove excess Ag+, NO3 –, PVP, glucose and its oxidation products.

Zeta potential measurements were inverstigated by Nano ZS/ZEN3690 (Malvern, UK). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The morphology and monodispersity of AgNPs/CDs were characterized through transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL Ltd, Japan). The solid powers were obtained by freeze dryers (Scientz-10 N, CHINA). Energy dispersive X-ray spectrum was performed on an AXIS ULTRA DLD spectrometer (Escalab 250xi). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was acquired on an FT-IR spectrometer (Agilent Cary 660, USA). The morphological changes of bacteria were monitored using the Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). The Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescent spectrophotometer (F97Pro, China).

The redox properties of AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs were measured using cyclic voltammetry with scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in 10 mM FeCl3 solution. Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG) was used to detect the generation of ROS[37,38]. SOSG can highly selectively bind singlet oxygen (SO) and the probe emits green fluorescence (emission wavelength at 525 nm). In general, SOSG (5 μM) was added to the aqueous of AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs (3 mL, 10 μg mL−1) and FL intensity was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

The antibacterial activity of AgNPs/CDs was tested against Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacterium) and Escherichia coli (Gram-negative bacterium). In addition, Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and Luria-Bertani agar were used as the medium. The bacteria were cultured in LB broth medium at 37 ℃, 200 rpm for 24 h. To visualize the antibacterial effect, CDs, AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs were separately added into test tubes and mixed with bacteria in LB borth medium. Then, 100 μL of bacteria mixed culture was placed on LB-agar plate and incubated in a constant temperature oven at 37 ℃, 200 rpm for 24 h. The image of the bacterial colonies was taken by a digital camera. The zone of inhibition test was performed according to the method described previously[39]. 100 μL of bacteria was inoculated on an agar medium plate, and a circular sterile disk with a diameter of 5 mm was placed. Also, to determine synergistic effects, each sterile disc was impregnated with 10 µL of nanoparticles. After 12 h of incubation at 37 °C, images of the agar plates were captured and the antibacterial efficacy of the samples was assessed based on the area of ​​the bacterial inhibition zone.

In order to determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of AgNPs/CDs. E. coli and S. aureus were adjusted to 105 CFU/mL using LB borth media. Then 100 μL bacteria suspension was treated with 100 μL solution of PBS and AgNPs/CDs (2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40 μg/mL). And suspension was incubated in 5 mL LB borth medium at 37 ℃, 200 rpm for 24 h. After incubation, the turbidity of the medium was record by a digital imaging. At the same time, 200 μL mixed suspension was placed on LB-agar plate and incubated for 24 h. After that, the number of bacterial colonies was observed by digital imaging. The fewer the number of bacterial colonies, the better the effects of antibacterial. Finally, the plate did not show any growth of bacteria was determined as the MIC of AgNPs/CDs. And the growth curve assay was carried out to observe the periodic growth of bacteria. The assay was prepared by pipetting 100 μL bacteria suspension mixed with different concentration of AgNPs/CDs (2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40 μg/mL) into a 96 well plate. The optical density (OD) of the prepared suspension were recorded at period intervals of 1 h and the changes in optical density corresponding to bacterial growth were recorded.

The morphology of each sample following modification has been observed by using transmission electron microscopy. The images of TEM results (Fig. 1 ) indicated that a large number of spherical nanoparticles were formed and the diamters of CDs, AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs were measured to be 5.23 ± 1.18 nm, 11.8 ± 1.26 nm and 12.41 ± 1.47 nm. The narrow size distribution indicated good solubility in solutions. With the resluts of HRTEM, the distance of clear lattice spaces were 0.21 nm and 0.22 nm for CDs and AgNPs, which is consistent with previous reports[40,41]. The nanostructure of nanochains was shown in the Fig. S1, carbon dots and silver nanoparticles were arranged at intervals. In order to confirm the structure of AgNPs/CDs, TEM and EDS were used to study the elemental composition and morphology. As shown in Fig. S2, the existence of silver and carbon elements proved the crosslinking structure of the composite. And the weight percentage of C and Ag is 5.39% and 94.61%, respectively. Then, EDS element mapping was used for imaging the individual positions of C and Ag elements. And the red and green regions shown in Fig. S2b demonstrated that the component was reconstructed from two nanostructures.

To further inversitage the properities of CDs, AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs, UV–vis absorption was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 , it has a broad absorption in the range of 200 to 400 nm and the intense absorption peak of CDs occurs at 244 nm that is resulted from the π-π stacking of carbon cores and aromatic ring structure during the preparation of CDs[42]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak of AgNPs observed at 422 nm because of surface plasmon resonance excitation phenomena of the AgNPs[43]. Compared with the absorption peak of AgNPs, the broad peak wavelength at 460 nm with a red shift indicated the formation of AgNPs/CDs[44].

In order to further understand the mechanism of the excellent antibacterial effect and the assembly process of AgNPs/CDs, all zeta potential values were shown in Fig. S3. The surface of bacteria is negatively charged due to a large number of acidic phosphate groups in the cell wall. And the zeta potential of AgNPs (-11.8 mV) and AgNPs/CDs (3.82 mV) indicated the suprior potetial of biosynthesized AgNPs/CDs to interact with negatively charged bacteria via electrosatic attraction, and released Ag+ which destroyed the full structure of bacterial to inhibit growth of microrganizes. And the positive potential of CDs of 14.5 mV was observed, which could be combined with AgNPs to form the nanochain of AgNPs/CDs[45].

Furthermore, X-ray photoeletron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to indetify characteristic functional groups and elemental composition of the AgNPs/CDs. The signals of C 1s, O 1s, Ag 3d and N 1s peaks of AgNPs/CDs have been observed in the spectra and the atomic ratios were calculated to 46.61%, 14.27%, 38.00% and 1.11% in Fig. 3 a, respectively. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of AgNPs/CDs were assigned to C = O (287.1 eV), C-O (286.0 eV), C-C (284.8 eV) and C-N (287.1 eV), indicating the presenece of lots of functional groups. The spectrum of the O 1s can be well fitted with the binding energy of C-O-C, C = O and C-O-H, which located at 532.7 eV, 533.1 eV and 532 eV, respectively[46,47]. The typical xps spetrum of Ag 3d was show in the Fig. 3d. The peaks at 368.4 eV and 374.4 eV correspond to the bind energy of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 [48]. Meanwhile, the two peaks of 367.3 eV and 373.3 eV could be assigned to the binding energy of Ag (0), which demonstrated that the sliver ions were reduced to metallic sliver and the conjugation between AgNPs and CDs[49,50].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used to identify the characteristic peaks and chemical structure of AgNPs/CDs. In Fig. 4 , the peaks of O-H and C-C chemical bonds were found at 1028 cm−1 and 1294 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of all the samples. And the peaks that occurred at 1653 cm−1 (C = C) and 635 cm−1 (C-N) can be observed in the FT-IR spectra of AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs[51]. These signals of the functional groups such as hydroxyl groups indicate the excellent water solubility of the sample. Additionally, it’s clear that the characteric peaks of AgNPs and CDs was contained in FT-IR spectrum of AgNPs/CDs, suggesting that the obtained AgNPs should be conjugated with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect of the Ag NPs/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The E. coli and S. aureus (109 CFU/mL) were used to investigated the antibacterial effect of nanocomposites AgNPs/CDs. And the antibacterial activity results treated with CDs, AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs (100 μg/mL) were shown in Fig. S4 by using LB medium test. It was clear that a large number of bacterial colonies were observed in the CDs group, indicating not antimicrobial effect toward S. aureus and E. coli for CDs. And there were several live bacteria colonies in the medium incubated with AgNPs. However, an obvious inhibition effect was observed in the AgNPs/CDs group with no colonies, which indicated the excellent antibacterial property of AgNPs/CDs.

To evaluate the growth inhibition effects of AgNPs/CDs on S. aureus and E. coli, the growth curves and zone of inhibition test were carried out. As shown in Fig. S5, the growth of bacteria was effectively inhibited by AgNPs/CDs and the clear inhibition zones with an average diameter of 8.77 mm and 9.80 mm can be observed on E. coli and S. aureus samples, respectively. Compared with Gram-negative bacteria, the growth of Gram-positive bacteria was significantly inhibited by AgNPs/CDs and it can be noted that the synergistic antibacterial effect of AgNPs/CDs was better than AgNPs. The growth kinetics of E. coli and S. aureus were similar to the results of inhibition zone assays. As shown in Fig. 5 , the growth of bacteria was inhibited by AgNPs/CDs at almost all concentrations. The different antibacterial effect of AgNPs/CDs on Gram- positive bacteria and Gram-negative bacteria may be due to the different cell wall structures of bacteria. With the positively charged on the surface, the binding interactions of AgNPs/CDs on the negatively charged bacteria were enhanced and the growth of bacteria was inhibited.

In order to determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of AgNPs/CDs, gram-negative (E. coli) and gram-positive (S. aureus) representative bacteria were incubated with AgNPs/CDs (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40 μg/mL). As shown in Fig. 6 , the number of bacterial colonies decreased gradually with the increasing concentration of AgNPs/CDs. The results demonstrated that all the pathogenic microbes were highly inhibited by 40 μg/mL of AgNPs/CDs. It’s worth noting that the AgNPs/CDs exhibited a better antibacterial effect against S. aureus than E. coli at 2.5 μg/mL and the growth activity of S. aureus was completely inhibited at 20 μg/mL. This is consistent with the SEM results. As shown in Fig. 7 , some intact E. coli with typically rod-like structure were found in the AgNPs/CDs sample. However, all S. aureus with some degree of deformation was observed in the group incubated with AgNPs/CDs. And the external surface of bacteria was covered by AgNPs/CDs which destroyed the cell wall and inhibited gene expression, further confirming desirable antibacterial effect of AgNPs/CDs.

To further explore the synergistic antibacterial effect of AgNPs/CDs, we compared the inhibition effect of AgNPs/CDs with commerical AgNPs and AuNPs for E. coli (Gram-negative bacterium) and S. aureus (Gram-positive bacterium) by using LB medium test. When E. coli and S. aureus were incubated with commerical nanomaterials at 30 μg/mL, desiriable colonies were caculated in the medium with commerical AgNPs and several colonies were caculated in the AuNPs group (in Fig. 8 ). However, there were no colonies in the sample incubated with AgNPs/CDs in the antibacterial test, which indicated the better antibacterial activity of AgNPs/CDs than commerical nanomaterial. These above results demonstrated excellent potential of AgNPs/CDs in antibacterial.

Oxidative stress induced by metal-mediated reactive oxygen species (ROS) is one of the major causes for the antibacterial effect of AgNPs[52]. In Fig. S6, the cyclic voltammograms show the excellent redox capacity of AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs. For AgNPs, the oxidation peak was exhibited at 0.594 V. However, the electrochemical response of AgNPs/CDs was dramatically enhanced with an oxidation peak at 0.661 V, which could be attributed to the struture of AgNPs/CDs. On the one hand, the efficiency separation of electrons and holes on the AgNPs/CDs was promoted by CDs with a large number of functional groups on the surface. On the other hand, AgNPs/CDs with the chain structure were crosslinked into sheets, and the electron transfer ability was accelerated. With the excellent redox properity of AgNPs and AgNPs/CDs, SOSG was used to evalute the capacity of generation ROS. Compared with AgNPs in Fig. S7, the higher green emission intensity was observed when SOSG was incubated with AgNPs/CDs, implying the efficiency of ROS production was enhanced after hybridization of CDs onto the AgNPs. As shown in Fig. 9 , it’s easy for positively charged AgNPs/CDs to be enriched on the surface of bacteria, and lots of ROS generated by AgNPs/CDs led to the death of bacteria.

In this work, a linearly arranged AgNPs/CDs was fabricated by situ growth and it was used to investigate the antibacterial effect against Gram’s negative and Gram’s positive pathogenic bacteria. The synergistic antibacterial ability of composite AgNPs/CDs was shown in LB antibacterial test. With the structure like nanochains, the positively charged AgNPs/CDs were enriched on the surface of negative charged bacteria, which promoted the generation of ROS to destroy the cell wall and kill the bacteria. Compared with commerial metal nanomaterials, there is no doubt that AgNPs/CDs with excellent antibacterial activity might have an enormous potential to resist the bacterial infections.

Panyong Wang: Project administration, Writing – original draft. Yizhi Song: Methodology, Validation, Data curation. Qian Mei: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Wen-Fei Dong: Writing – review & editing. Li Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21803075, 91959112, 81902166, 62027825, and 82172077), the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2022326), the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 2020SYHZ0041), the Instrument Developing Project of Chinese Academy of Science (YJKYYQ20200038), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.154125.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tackling antibiotic resistance: the environmental framework

Stefani, T. Schwartz, V. Kisand, F. Baquero, J.L. Martinez, Tackling antibiotic resistance: the environmental framework, Nat Rev Microbiol, 13 (2015) 310-317.

C. Neu Harold, The Crisis in Antibiotic Resistance, Science, 257 (1992) 1064-1073.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterial-based therapeutics for antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial mechanisms of graphene-based composite nanomaterials
